title: Specificity and source order

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling draft for Specificity and source order; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Selector specificity compares id, class-like, and type-like components lexicographically after higher cascade factors tie.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A component class beats a type selector without needing an id or !important escalation. Your goal is to explain and repair a specificity conflict without adding unnecessary important declarations. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: html document syntax; Origins, importance and layers. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Selector specificity compares id, class-like, and type-like components lexicographically after higher cascade factors tie. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: The :where() pseudo-class contributes zero specificity, :is() uses its most specific argument, and inline styles have their own position. This draft therefore rejects: Specificity is one total number where ten classes always equal one id.

includes: Calculate selector specificity and prefer maintainable ordering over escalation.
Explain and repair a specificity conflict without adding unnecessary important declarations.
excludes: The :where() pseudo-class contributes zero specificity, :is() uses its most specific argument, and inline styles have their own position.

outcomes: Explain and repair a specificity conflict without adding unnecessary important declarations.

examples.title: Specificity and source order: concrete case
examples.situation: A component class beats a type selector without needing an id or !important escalation.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A component class beats a type selector without needing an id or !important escalation.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Treat specificity as compared components, not base-ten arithmetic, and keep component selectors intentionally low.
examples.result: The result follows because Treat specificity as compared components, not base-ten arithmetic, and keep component selectors intentionally low.

examples.title: Specificity and source order: boundary case
examples.situation: The :where() pseudo-class contributes zero specificity, :is() uses its most specific argument, and inline styles have their own position.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: The :where() pseudo-class contributes zero specificity, :is() uses its most specific argument, and inline styles have their own position.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Specificity is one total number where ten classes always equal one id.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Treat specificity as compared components, not base-ten arithmetic, and keep component selectors intentionally low.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Specificity is one total number where ten classes always equal one id.
misconceptions.correction: Treat specificity as compared components, not base-ten arithmetic, and keep component selectors intentionally low.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling artifact to explain why this belief fails: Specificity is one total number where ten classes always equal one id.

analogy.example: A layered stage design can model Specificity and source order, where rules size, place, and paint existing content.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Specificity and source order before using exact platform terms
limitations: Physical layers do not reproduce cascade algorithms, intrinsic sizing, writing modes, or user preferences.
